data.title: Visa's Valuation Looks High - Until You Run The Numbers
data.description:
My last article about Visa, Inc. (NYSE:V) was published more than three years ago. Back then, I rated Visa as a cautious “Buy” and wrote in my conclusion:

I am still a bit cautious about Visa as the stock is still trading for a rather high valuation multiple and the company must continue growing with a high pace to justify the current valuation multiple. But when forced to make a call about Visa, I would be cautiously bullish at this point and Visa could be a great long-term investment when buying at current prices. On the other hand, I still expect lower prices for the stock in the coming quarters (or years) and the ability to purchase Visa cheaper (but that is only speculation).

During that timeframe, Visa’s stock price increased 63% (and about 66% when including dividends) while the S&P 500 increased 59% in the same timeframe. Hence, Visa performed in line with the overall market, but we should not ignore that the overall market performed exceptionally in the last three years.

I personally did not invest in Visa back then as the stock was not cheap enough in my opinion. In the following article, I will analyze Visa once again and answer the question of whether a 60% stock price increase is justified. I will argue that Visa's wide economic moat combined with constant innovation is driving top-line growth, and high amounts spent on share buybacks are adding to even higher bottom-line growth. This is justifying high valuation multiples and Visa remains a "Buy" despite trading close to all-time highs.

Quarterly Results

As always, we start by looking at the last reported results. On July 29, 2025, Visa reported third quarter results for fiscal 2025 and the company did beat on revenue as well as earnings per expectations once again. Visa exceeded revenue estimates by $350 million, and non-GAAP earnings per share beat expectations by $0.13.

Net revenue increased from $8,900 million in Q3/24 to $10,172 million in Q3/25 – resulting in 14.3% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 4.0% year-over-year from $5,938 million in the same quarter last year to $6,177 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $2.40 in Q3/24 to $2.69 in Q3/25 – resulting in 12.1% year-over-year growth for the bottom line.

Share Buybacks

When asking whether Visa can continue to grow and at what pace it might grow in the years to come, share buybacks are an important contributor to bottom-line growth. Visa is actually spending huge amounts of its free cash flow on share buybacks and while overall growth is still stemming in huge parts from top line growth, share buybacks are also contributing.

Visa is constantly buying back shares, and in the last ten years, Visa lowered the number of diluted shares outstanding with a CAGR of 2.17%. In the recent past, share buybacks were even accelerated and in the last twelve months, the number of diluted shares outstanding declined from 2,029 million twelve months ago to 1,959 million – resulting in a decrease of 3.4%.

Share buybacks were playing an important role and Visa spent $58 billion on share buybacks between 2020 and 2024. Additionally, Visa is paying $17 billion in dividends between 2020 and 2024 and Visa is targeting a payout ratio of 20% to 25%. Right now, Visa is paying a quarterly dividend of $0.59, resulting in an annual dividend of $2.36 and a dividend yield of only 0.7%.

Growing The Top Line

Visa will not only grow by using share buybacks as a capital allocation tool, but the biggest part of growth will stem from growing its revenue. One of the huge advantages for Visa as a business is the fact that Visa does not have to reinvent its business constantly and it doesn’t have to come up with completely new products or new ways to generate revenue.

Visa must improve its one major existing product or asset: the very base layer of global connectivity of 150 million points of merchant sale as well as connectivity to almost 5 billion credentials. The company is building new services like micro services for issuer processing, acceptance processing or risk management services.

These are often improvements that might seem small – like “tap” – but can lead to higher revenue for the business. These innovations don’t change the core business but make it slightly better. During the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference, the Chief Product & Strategy Officer Jack Forestell made the following statement:

Billions of people love to Tap. It's the gold standard for a payment transaction. But we're still on a journey. There's still a lot of room to grow. We're at 78% penetration at the physical point of sale for Tap transactions. And I'll remind you, when we get users converting to Tap, we see meaningful lifts in cash conversion and meaningful lifts in spend per active card. It's like once you try that Tap, you're connected to it. It's an easier way to go and you just start doing it for everything that you possibly can.

We're only, though, I say there's upside. We're only at 63% in the United States. We've come a long way very quickly, but there's still 37% to go in the U.S. And just to give you a sense of the momentum.

Additionally, Visa can also use AI to improve its business. For value-added services like fraud detection, AI can certainly help to develop new tools and also increase the level of automation for customers. AI can also be used to standardize some other processes and during the last earnings call, Jack Forestell talked about the authorization process:

You might say, well, Jack, authorization has been around for a very, very long time. Surely, there can't be more there, but there is. We're building new capabilities like our AI-based stand-in processing. We have a capability that allows issuers to set up rules and configure authorization for them as we stand in for them sometimes. We're replacing that with self-learning algorithms that can learn how issuers process their own authorizations and stand that in to create a more fit-for-purpose capability. That's just an example. We do the same thing across dispute resolution, across tokenization, the whole gamut of things.

Cryptocurrency, and especially stablecoins, seems like another growth opportunity for Visa. Since 2020, the company enabled crypto users to spend more than $25 billion in Bitcoin, Ethereum and other cryptocurrencies. In the last earnings call, management was talking a lot about stablecoins and how they have been active in this space for almost a decade and believe stablecoins can solve important payment problems for certain users. Management was also pointing out how stablecoins might be an opportunity for emerging markets:

In the emerging market use case I mentioned earlier, consumers and businesses are using stablecoins to save money in U.S. dollars. But they also want easy and safe ways to spend that money, and there's no better way to do that than using a Visa card. Stablecoin-linked Visa cards are an extension of what we have been doing in the crypto space for years. Since 2020, we have enabled crypto users to spend more than $25 billion in Bitcoin, Ethereum and an array of other cryptocurrencies, and now stablecoins. We are also enabling cross-border money movement capabilities for P2P and B2B in certain emerging markets. And we are piloting and partnering with stablecoin payments companies who specialize in these markets as we build out our stablecoin treasury stack for settlement and money movement flows.

Visa has a huge total addressable market – starting with currently $41 trillion consumer spending (and less than half of these payments are currently processed by global card networks). Visa also identified about $200 trillion in new money flows that could be processed by Visa as well. And finally, value added services are also a gigantic market with over $500 billion in potential revenue.

One simple way to grow (to address the untapped revenue potential) is by issuing new cards. Year-over-year, the number of total issued cards is increasing about 7% and although this is not automatically leading to 7% (as the payment volume of these newly issued cards might be lower at the beginning), it is certainly a major contributor to growth.

All this leads to high expected growth rates for Visa. Management itself does not offer a detailed long-term guidance, but it is talking about compelling EPS growth.

Analysts are being more specific and for the next few years, they are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 13.1%. For the upcoming fourth quarter, analysts are also expecting continued growth and revenue is expected to be $10.60 billion (resulting in about 10% top line growth) and earnings per share are expected to be $2.94 (resulting in only about 6% for the bottom line).

High-Quality Business

The main reason why Visa does not have to invent new products and probably would be able to afford not being innovative for a few years without losing a lot of business is the wide economic moat. If anything, customers might switch more to Mastercard, and hence Mastercard is forcing Visa to be innovative – and vice versa. But right now, it seems extremely unlikely that a new outside competitor might enter the business and be able to compete. Visa, being one of the most valuable brands in the world, is certainly helping, but especially the high switching costs and network effects between merchants and customers are making Visa such a successful business.

We already wrote in the past about the wide economic moat Visa has and three years after my last article was published, we can only reaffirm this assessment, although we should point out that Visa underperformed the S&P 500 in the last five years. However, in the last ten years Visa clearly outperformed the S&P 500 and increased 365% in price compared to 225% increase for the index. And when looking at data since the IPO, the outperformance of Visa is even more obvious (2,330% vs. 405%)

The stock price performance is not the best metric anyway as valuation multiples play a huge role whether a stock is outperforming the overall market, and this is rather influenced by sentiment than by fundamentals. Instead, it is better to look at other metrics like the gross margin and operating margin, which are extremely stable in case of Visa. This is a strong sign for a high quality business and pricing power.

Aside from very stable (and high) margins, Visa is also reporting an impressive return on invested capital. In the last ten years, Visa reported an average return on invested capital of 22.90% and in the last five years it even reported an average ROIC of 27.15%.

Only about 10% of companies between 1963 and 2023 reached a return on invested capital similar to Visa – and this is therefore putting Visa among the best companies out there.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We established once again that Visa is a great business with stable margins and very high return on invested capital. Visa is also growing with high stability and consistency in the double-digits and an extremely profitable business. Hence, it all comes down to the stock price once again – whether Visa is a good investment or not.

When looking at the two simple valuation multiples – the price-earnings ratio and the price-free-cash-flow ratio – we can certainly state that Visa is not a cheap stock and trades for valuation multiples above 30. At the time of writing, Visa is trading for 30.8 times free cash flow and for 33.9 times earnings. Both metrics are below the 10-year average, indicating that Visa is not overvalued at this point and might be at least fairly valued.

However, a better metric to determine whether Visa is a good investment or not is a discounted cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate and the last reported number of shares outstanding (1,959 million in Q3/25). As basis, we use the free cash flow over the last four quarters, which was $22,080 million. In order to be fairly valued, Visa must grow 9% annually for the next ten years, followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. And it seems like Visa is certainly capable of growing at such a pace, making the stock at least fairly valued (maybe even undervalued).

But we must look at several metrics in more detail. First, I am calculating with a growth rate of 6% till perpetuity, and usually it is recommended to calculate only with a terminal growth rate of 2% to 4% ( according to the CFI). In the case of Visa, I would make a strong argument that we can assume Visa outperforming the general economy for a very long time, and a terminal growth rate of 6% might be justified for a business that is reporting very stable margins and extremely high ROIC over a long timeframe.

Additionally, we look at the growth rate in the next ten years, and 9% annual growth certainly seems manageable for Visa. Not only was Visa growing at a much higher pace in the last 10 years (revenue grew with a CAGR of 10.96% and earnings per share grew with a CAGR of 16.27%), but analysts are also expecting Visa to grow with a CAGR of 13% in the next few years.

Finally, we must look at the free cash flow in the last four quarters, as this is the number we use as a basis in our calculation. Of course, free cash flow is at a record high right now, and we should question if this is the number we should use. However, we can point out that Visa is constantly growing at a solid pace. We can look at the FCF margin and see that Visa is reporting a margin clearly above the long-term average, which was 43.4%. Right now, Visa is reporting an impressive free cash flow margin of 56.8%. We can look at the last ten years, and Visa is reporting a free cash flow margin of 51.7%.

A Few Risks

Despite the wide economic moat protecting Visa’s business, we should not ignore potential risks for the company. While it might be extremely difficult for competitors to overcome the moat Visa (as well as Mastercard) have, the situation is different for regulators. An administration (this one or a future one) might try to pass a new piece of legislation to limit the grip Visa and Mastercard have over the credit card payment industry. Or legislators could try to reduce (or forbid) certain types of fees Visa and Mastercard can charge, which can have a huge impact on the top and bottom line.

Another potential risk might arise from cryptocurrencies. Especially as Donald Trump seems to be a massive supporter of crypto, efforts to make cryptocurrencies more mainstream and widely used forms of payment in the United States have to be seen as a possibility.

Conclusion

In my opinion, we can make the argument that Visa is a bit undervalued and probably a long-term “Buy”. Without much doubt, Visa is a high-quality business with a wide economic moat around the business and the ability to constantly report double-digit growth rates and very high levels of stability and consistency.

However, we should point out that Visa is also at risk for steeper declines. If the U.S. economy might enter a recession or Visa stumbling a bit for some different reason, the stock might decline quickly as investors could suddenly assume that a valuation multiple between 30 and 35 is too high and a lower valuation multiple leads to a lower stock price for Visa.

data.title: Reckitt Benckiser: Stock Price Gains, Fundamentals Lag
data.description:
Five months ago, in May 2025, I published my last article about Reckitt Benckiser Group Plc (OTCPK:RBGPF) and I rated the stock as a “Hold”. Although I was slightly optimistic about the business and saw limited downside at that point, I did not rate the stock as a “Buy”. Since my last article was published, Reckitt Benckiser actually performed quite well and the stock increased 20% (including dividends), which is a very good performance over a timeframe of five months. Of course, we must point out that the overall market performed quite well during that timeframe. Nevertheless, Reckitt Benckiser outperformed the S&P 500 (which increased about 14% in value) as well as the FTSE 100 (which increased about 10.5%).

Last time I was slightly optimistic - especially due to the limited downside I saw for the stock - but now I am cautious about Reckitt Benckiser as an investment. That caution is based on two main arguments: First, as mentioned above, the stock is now trading 20% higher and this is shifting the risk-reward profile for investors towards a lower reward. Second, Reckitt Becnkiser is still missing a clear path towards solid and sustainable growth, although the divestiture of the Essential Home business is eliminating one of the reasons for the mediocre results.

Disappointing Results...

On July 24, 2025, Reckitt Benckiser reported results for the first six months of fiscal 2025 and results were rather disappointing once again. Net revenue declined from GBP 7,167 million in H1/24 to GBP 6,981 million in H1/25 – resulting in a 2.6% year-over-year decline. Operating profit also declined 10.7% year-over-year from GBP 1,678 million in the first half of 2024 to GBP 1,498 in the first half of this year. Diluted earnings per share declined from 161.0 pence in H1/24 to 139.8 pence in H1/25 – resulting in a 13.2% year-over-year bottom line growth.

Finally, free cash flow declined from GBP 821 million in the first six months of 2024 to GBP 623 million in the first six months of 2025 – a decline of 24.1% year-over-year. Free cash flow conversion also declined from 72% in H1/24 to only 54% in H1/25.

…But Only On The Surface

So far, we get the picture of Reckitt Benckiser continuing to struggle and having trouble growing. And it certainly seems to be true that the results are not great, but when looking at the results in more detail, the picture is a little different.

Of course, we should always be cautious about adjusted results. Nevertheless, Reckitt Benckiser’s like-for-like growth was 1.5% in the first half of 2025, and operating profit increased 1.8% year-over-year. When adjusted operating profit for FX effects, it grew even 7.0% and finally, adjusted diluted earnings per share increased 4.4%.

I also mentioned already in my last article that Reckitt Benckiser is focusing on its core business and trying to divest parts of its business. And when we only look at the Core Reckitt business segment, we see all four reporting like-for-like growth. While Self Care is reporting only 0.7% growth in the second quarter, Germ Protection is growing 8.4% and Intimate Wellness was growing 10.6%. When looking at the first half of 2025, Self Care still reported a like-for-like decline, but the other three segments are also positive.

Aside from the Core Reckitt, the company has two other non-core business segments: Mead Johnson Nutrition and Essential Home. In July 2025, it was reported that Reckitt Benckiser entered into an agreement with Advent International to sell its Essential Home business for an enterprise value of up to $4.8 billion. And Essential Home is certainly one of the reasons for Reckitt Benckiser having to report a decline in the top and bottom line. In the second quarter as well as the first half of 2025, the business reported about 6% like-for-like decline, with volume as well as price contributing in a negative way.

Reckitt Benckiser will keep a 30% equity stake in Essential Home, which includes global brands like Air Wick, Calgon, Woolite, Cillit Bang and many different other global brands, which are sold in over 70 markets. And the cash Reckitt Benckiser will receive from the selling of Essential Home can be used to reduce the company’s debt, and management announced “balance sheet adjustments.” However, about $2.2 billion will be distributed to shareholders as a special dividend following completion.

The second non-core business is Mead Johnson Nutrition, which is still struggling and declined 6.2% like-for-like in the second quarter. During the last earnings call, management commented:

In Mead Johnson Nutrition, the business continues to recover well from the impacts of the Mount Vernon tornado last year. We've delivered continued recovery in North American market share and good performance across our international markets. Our stated intention for Mead Johnson remains unchanged, and we continue to consider all strategic options for the business.

Emerging Markets Outperforming

Reckitt Benckiser continues to outperform in emerging markets. While revenue was flat in Europe (when adjusted for FX effects), and in North America revenue declined even 2.7% like-for-like, emerging markets reported 14.9% like-for-like growth in the second quarter of fiscal 2025, with volume as well as price/mix contributing each about 7.5% to these strong double-digit growth rates.

When looking at the numbers for the first half of 2025, the picture is very similar, and we can even see the situation improving in the second quarter, which is a good sign. During the last earnings call, CFO Shannon Sue Eisenhardt pointed especially towards India and China for high growth rates:

Looking now at volumes, where we've achieved a sequential improvement across Core Reckitt through the half and continue to drive a more balanced volume price growth algorithm. Emerging Markets continued to see strong volumes, up over 7% in Q2, driven by Dettol in India and China, Intimate Wellness in China and across Latin America as it lapped a softer comparative period. In Europe, we saw sequential improvement in volumes, helped by Finish gaining market leadership positions across all its largest markets.

Aside from pointing towards India and China, it is especially Durex (among some other powerbrands) where management sees huge potential. During Barclays 18th Annual Global Consumer Staples Conference, CEO Kris Licht stated:

So I think Durex is maybe one of those brands that exemplify what we talked about before, which is the power of strong R&D and breakthrough innovation. We have really made big strides in our IP, our capabilities as it pertains to materials innovation. And in condoms, it's really all about materials and what benefits they bring. And so we are now clearly global leaders again, and that's why I want us to stay.

We have a big runway for growth. It's actually not just China. I mean, Durex is growing double digit in many Emerging Markets and it's got a big runway for growth in Emerging Markets, kind of similar to Finish. I mean, really, the category needs to be built and we're the right people to build it.

Mixed Growth Expectations

The company updated its 2025 guidance and is now expecting Reckitt Benckiser to grow revenue 3% to 4% (on a like-for-like basis) while in a previous guidance management was only expecting 2% to 4%. The “Core Reckitt” is now expected to grow above 4% (compared to the previous guidance of 3% to 4% like-for-like growth). Essential Home is remaining problematic and the business is expecting revenue to grow in the low-single digits (compared to low-single digit growth before).

In the end, Reckitt Benckiser is guiding for another year of EPS growth, which seems like a low bar to clear. But while Reckitt Benckiser is only slightly optimistic for the near-term future, management has higher ambitions for the mid-to-long-term future. For the mid-term, net revenue is expected to grow 4% to 5% on a like-for-like basis.

Analysts, on the other hand, seem to be less optimistic and assume revenue to grow “only” with a CAGR of 3.33% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2034. This is clearly in contrast to management being very optimistic. While double-digit growth is seen as rather unlikely, management is guiding towards high single digit growth in the years to come:

Yes. What we've said about this, and I believe that to still be true is we shouldn't get used to growing at double digit just because it's unlikely to sustain itself quarter in/quarter out, year in/year out. But high single digit, I feel very comfortable we can do consistently. And as we can see, the business can definitely do more than that, too, as it has been doing now for 3 or 4 quarters.

And Reckitt Benckiser will probably be able to grow its bottom line with a higher pace than the top lines as improving margins will also contribute:

Taking this together, we now expect to see group net revenue like-for-like growth in the 3% to 4% range. We expect to drive adjusted operating profit ahead of net revenue growth with Fuel for Growth benefits enabling us to continue to increase investment behind our brands and to sustainably increase margins.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

When looking at Reckitt Benckiser, we still have a company that continues to struggle but might slowly start improving when looking parts of the business. But to determine whether the stock is a good investment, we cannot only look at the business but also have to look at the stock price in relation to the fundamental business. At the time of writing, Reckitt Benckiser is trading for a P/E ratio of 32.7, which seems rather expensive. Looking at the price-free-cash-flow ratio, the picture is improving a bit as the stock is trading only for a valuation multiple of 20.

But here it is getting a bit difficult. The valuation multiples Reckitt Benckiser is trading for are certainly not indicating that the stock is a bargain and solid growth is certainly necessary to justify 20 times earnings and especially to justify 33 times earnings. However, management is constantly pointing out that reported results might not be the best metrics to use and rather look at adjusted numbers and exclude parts of the business.

As always, we should rather use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value as it enables us to better account for such fluctuations and a free cash flow that might not be representative for the business in the years to come. Before we start calculating, let’s also calculate a WACC for the business. Using common assumptions (a risk-free rate in line with the 10-year treasury yield and a market risk premium of 6%) as well as the last reported numbers, we get a WACC of 5.77% for Reckitt Benckiser.

Aside from using that discount rate in a first calculation, we calculate with 685.2 million diluted shares outstanding and the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was GBP 2,034 million. In this scenario, Reckitt Benckiser must grow its free cash flow only between 0% and 1% to be fairly valued and we therefore could make the case that Reckitt Benckiser is a good investment.

However, I would argue once again that we should calculate with a discount rate of 10% as this is the annual return I like to generate. It certainly makes sense to account for different risk profiles of different businesses, but I rather like to “normalize” the intrinsic value calculation and then decide whether I invest in a business or not. In this scenario, Reckitt Benckiser must grow its free cash flow about 5% annually to be fairly valued. And when being a little more cautious and assume only 4% growth till perpetuity. For the next ten years, Reckitt Benckiser must grow between 6% and 7% in order to be fairly valued.

At this point it might make sense to make another adjustment. After selling the Essential Home business (and keeping only a 30% stake), free cash flow for Reckitt Benckiser will be reduced by probably GBP 200 million (we can only make estimates here, but other sources are seeing the numbers in a similar range). This would lead to a free cash flow of GBP 1,800 million to GBP 1,850 million and Reckitt Benckiser must grow 8% annually for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity.

Even when making different assumptions in our calculation here, the conclusion probably is that Reckitt Benckiser is fairly valued at best.

Upcoming Trading Update

Next week, Reckitt Benckiser will report third-quarter results. And as always, this is only a trading update and not a full earnings release (like we can expect from companies in the United States), and the information is limited.

Nevertheless, I like to see Reckitt Benckiser improving its business once again. We certainly can exclude the results reported by Essential Home and should not be bothered if Reckitt Benckiser will report a like-for-like decline again (which seems likely). On the other hand, the core business should improve again, and the best case would be all four segments growing on a like-for-like basis and growth accelerating a bit. I also would like margins to improve again (although information about margins during a trading update is usually sparse).

Overall, I would like to see Reckitt Benckiser being able to stop its revenue declining although both Essential Home as well as Mead Johnson Nutrition might continue to be a drag for the business and report like-for-like declines.

Conclusion

I certainly see signs for improvement, and Reckitt Benckiser seems to be able to grow at a high pace in its emerging markets. Additionally, its four core business segments also seem to be able to grow, and it is justified to exclude the Essential Home business from results as it will be sold in the foreseeable future.

On the other hand, we should always be cautious about using adjusted numbers, and revenue as well as free cash flow continues to struggle. With the stock price now trading 20% higher compared to my last article, Reckitt Benckiser certainly is a “Hold” at this point, and I also see some downside risk again. In the upcoming trading update next week, Reckitt Benckiser clearly must demonstrate that the business is continuing to improve.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Silver's Price Consolidates Ahead Of Next Move (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Silver prices continue to soar to unprecedented highs, with questions being asked about the reason for the rally. Well, in all honesty, there have been a host of reasons cited as a driving force; all of them may be true to some degree.

The most popular ones that have been discussed at length include rate cut expectations from the Federal Reserve, the ongoing supply/demand deficit in physical silver, and, of course, the price of silver being cheap in comparison to gold.

One of the reasons that has really come to the fore recently is the shortage of physical silver, which has led to a big premium for physical silver as well. Recent, widely reported incidents have exposed some key factors regarding silver, particularly due to physical shortages that have made the metal difficult to acquire. This is becoming a global problem.

This shortage is especially felt in India, the world's biggest consumer, which has seen its imports drop by a significant 42% this year, even as demand from both investors and industrial users (like those making solar panels and electronics) has surged.

The problem is amplified globally because most silver is produced as a side product of mining other metals, making it difficult to quickly increase supply when demand spikes.

As a result, dealers everywhere are struggling to find the metal, and this scarcity is driving up prices in the supply chain. This physical shortage is not limited to India; countries including China, Turkey, and Australia are also currently facing a scarcity of silver.

As the physical silver shortage continues, the amount of money held in silver exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and futures contracts has surged. Large investment funds are now viewing silver as a "higher beta" version of an inflation hedge, meaning it's more volatile than gold, but offers the chance for much larger gains when the market moves up.

This structural shift is driven by the fact that silver offers dual benefits that gold does not:

  1. Monetary Asset: Like gold, it protects against the long-term devaluation of traditional paper money (monetary debasement).
  2. Industrial Asset: It acts as a powerful bet on industrial growth and the global "energy transition" theme, as silver is a crucial, irreplaceable material used in fast-growing sectors like solar panels, electric vehicles, and high-tech electronics.

This unique combination makes silver attractive to both traditional commodity investors looking for a hedge and other market participants focused on clean energy trends. Either way, right now these factors have created the perfect cocktail for silver prices.

Technical Analysis - Silver (XAG/USD)

From a technical standpoint, silver has settled into a period of consolidation since the early hours of Wednesday morning. Price is just shy of the recent high print around the 53.62/oz handle, with the period-14 RSI above the 50 level. This is a nod to how strong the bullish momentum behind the silver move is.

Similar to gold, picking a top at this stage appears counterproductive. However, for day traders, opportunities may yet present themselves.

Silver (XAG/USD) H4 Chart, October 16, 2025

Dropping down to an H1 chart, price has been consolidating in the red/pink block since yesterday. A candle close outside this block could lead to a move in that direction. Obviously, the longer the price remains in the block, the more aggressive the breakout may be.

A break to the downside may find support at the 100-day MA resting at 51.84 before the October 14 swing low at 50.59, with the 200-day MA resting below that at the 50.28 handle. A break to the upside may find some resistance at the YTD high at 53.62 before the psychological 55.00 handle comes into focus.

Silver (XAG/USD) H1 Chart, October 16, 2025

Client Sentiment Data - USD/CAD

Looking at OANDA client sentiment data, market participants are long on XAG/USD, with 64% of traders net-long. I prefer to take a contrarian view toward crowd sentiment, and thus the fact that so many traders are long means XAG/USD prices could fall in the near term.

Original Post

data.title: StubHub Holdings: Attractive Upside If It Can Continue To Execute Well
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for StubHub Holdings (NYSE:STUB), as I believe the growth potential is not fully appreciated. StubHub is a leading player in the secondary ticket marketplace, and it is also moving into the primary ticketing space. This move, along with the advertising revenue stream, extends its growth runway and should drive up margins. If all these play out as I expected, I see material upside from current levels.

Company description

StubHub operates a global online ticket marketplace for live events, connecting buyers and sellers across more than 200 countries. It has two primary brands. StubHub, which is its flagship in North America, and viagogo, which serves international markets. While its core business is the secondary, or resale, ticket market, STUB is also expanding into the primary ticketing market through its "Direct Issuance" initiative. This solution allows teams, artists, and venues to sell their original inventory directly to the public on its marketplace.

A Massive and Expanding TAM

STUB operates in a very large and growing market, one that is benefiting from the shift where consumers are prioritizing experiences over goods. And when it comes to experiences, live events/concerts are direct beneficiaries, which by definition also means more ticket sales/purchase occasions. These are incremental demand opportunities for STUB, especially as it expands to cover primary ticketing.

STUB’s core business, secondary ticketing, will benefit because a more vibrant primary market directly fuels the secondary one. My idea is that the more popular and in-demand an event is at the initial sale, the greater the subsequent need for a resale market. This happens for two simple reasons. First, not everyone who wants a ticket can get one at the primary ticketing phase, and this creates demand from fans who missed out. Second, life is unpredictable. Fans who purchase tickets months in advance often may find they can no longer attend due to changed plans, and this creates a natural supply of tickets that would otherwise go to waste. Therefore, you can think of the secondary market as a clearinghouse, one that matches this supply with the unmet demand. As the overall pie of live events grows, so does the volume of these transactions, which creates a larger resale ecosystem.

Widening its Moat

Within the secondary ticketing market, StubHub is a leader, and there are hard numbers to back this up. According to management estimates, STUB held ~42% of the North American resale market in 2024, which is sizeable. For better perspective, STUB facilitated the sale of >40 million tickets from >1 million unique sellers in 2024, generating $8.7 billion in gross sales. And perhaps the last data point that proves STUB is a leader is its brand strength, which has 84% aided awareness among US consumers.

If any of you have done research on a marketplace model business before, you understand that scale changes everything. It is very simple to understand how this is the case. A broad ticket inventory attracts more buyers, which draws in more sellers, and that expands the ticket inventory. This is a flywheel effect that makes STUB the platform for both sides of the marketplace. Some may push back by saying that event organizers can still list their events on other platforms, even if there is little consumer demand (the idea is to capture whatever demand is out there). I don’t disagree that this may happen, but those would be less popular events anyway. I would think big event organizers would just use STUB’s platform, as it is easier to manage 1 platform vs. a dozen of them, and STUB itself already provides access to 84% of consumers. This network effect gets better as STUB grows, which further supports its leading position.

Trust and Brand

In the ticketing world, trust is everything, and STUB has managed to win the trust of buyers and sellers. This trust is anchored by STUB’s FanProtect Guarantee, which ensures every ticket is valid for entry, or the buyer gets their money back. In a market where fraud and counterfeit tickets are real, this policy is a huge differentiator that removes purchase friction and improves conversion. There is also a flywheel effect here. As these buyers gain more confidence, the more they come back, and the more sellers want to list where those buyers already are.

Brand matters as well. The brand awareness metric I noted above is not just a marketing metric; it actually helps STUB to lower customer acquisition costs (CAC) and drive higher loyalty. On CAC, because consumers already know to look for STUB when they want to buy secondary tickets, this led to a large portion of STUB traffic being organic, which is a massive advantage over smaller players. Sub-scale competitors would need to rely on paid search just to get noticed, while STUB attracts millions of buyers to its platform for free.

Two New Drivers for Growth

STUB also has two other growth drivers that make the growth outlook even more exciting. These are direct issuance and advertising.

The Direct Issuance initiative is basically STUB’s primary ticketing offering. By allowing rights holders (sports teams, artists, and festivals) to list primary tickets directly on Stub’s marketplace, Stub effectively unlocks an entirely new profit pool in primary and unsold ticket inventory (those tickets that rights holders failed to sell). The early signs are highly encouraging. Partnerships with big names such as the New York Yankees, Los Angeles Dodgers, and a league-wide deal with Major League Baseball demonstrate that the model works and is gaining traction.

As for advertising, this is also a strong growth driver. There are already millions of consumers coming to STUB’s platform, and they are basically monetizable “assets”. Note that these are consumers with high-purchasing-intent, and hence, they are exactly the targets that brands want to reach. There are two steps here. The first is sponsored listings, where sellers can pay to feature their inventory at the top of search results. This gives them immediate visibility at the moment when fans are ready to buy. The second, and potentially more lucrative, layer is brand partnerships. As I said, STUB’s audience is what marketers want. These are consumers with disposable income, making them ideal targets for airlines, hotels, credit cards, and restaurants. The impact of this advertising is meaningful because advertising is inherently high-margin. Every incremental ad dollar drops almost entirely to the bottom line.

Valuation

Given that STUB just IPO-ed, I used consensus FY25 estimates as my baseline (already includes one-off expenses for all the IPO-related stuff). From FY25, my outlook is that STUB can accelerate growth back to >20% over time, riding on the secular trend, as the growth drivers (advertising and primary ticketing offering) gain traction. The next important assumption is that STUB should see significant margin inflection. If we compare against Live Nation (LYV) ticketing segment adj. operating income margin, which is at >30%, there is plenty of room for STUB to expand as well. Add on the advertising revenue stream that carries high margin, and it makes the path for margin expansion even more plausible. Say that STUB exits at a 30% adj. EBITDA margin; this business should do $855 million adj. EBITDA in FY28. Given that both STUB and LYV are exposed to the same secular tailwind and STUB is also going into primary ticketing, I believe the current valuation gap shouldn’t exist. Valuing STUB at 14x FY28 EBITDA (LYV current forward EBITDA multiple) would give us a share price target of $26.

Risk

While STUB has built a strong reputation of trust, a series of fraudulent activities will still hurt that brand, and that will give competitors an opening to capture share. Financially, STUB also operates with a significant amount of debt, and if growth doesn’t happen as I expect, this leverage could constrain its flexibility to reinvest. Also, any major negative macro event (like covid or a recession) will likely lead to a decline in live event activities, which would hurt growth.

Conclusion

My view on STUB is a buy rating. This business has a strong position in a large and growing market. Over the years of operating, STUB has built scale, brand trust, and a recurring organic traffic pool. These advantages should continue to get better as STUB scales. Meanwhile, execution on direct issuance and advertising should meaningfully accelerate growth and drive margin expansion. Therefore, I believe STUB’s stock is attractive.

data.title: YETI: Beware Sales Deterioration
data.description:
With the stock market increasingly showing signs of weakness near all-time highs, there has never been a better time to focus on active portfolio management and eliminating the stocks that aren’t performing up to scratch. The underlying macroeconomic reality, after all, is weaker than the rallying stock market might suggest.

YETI (NYSE:YETI), the maker of coolers and drinkware, is looking increasingly vulnerable as its wholesale partners pull back on replenishment orders. The company’s high gross margins have protected its bottom line in the tariff economy, but at the same time, we can’t ignore the company’s sinking sales profile. Down nearly 15% since the start of the year, I’m afraid YETI has more to lose.

I last wrote a sell article for YETI in July, when the stock was trading around 10% higher at ~$35 per share. Since then, the company has kept losing momentum among core U.S. demographics, though it has maintained a fair earnings forecast. Given the lack of growth catalysts this stock possesses amid a questionable demand environment, however, I’m reiterating my "Sell" rating on this stock.

The first point we should address is YETI’s sideways guidance update. The latest outlook is shown below:

YETI is now expecting 0%-2% sales growth this year, adjusted for the 53-week fiscal year comparison, down from a previous outlook of 1%-4% growth set in Q1 (which in itself was a cut from a prior 5%-7% growth expectation).

This guidance cut reflects a swift reduction in the company’s confidence. Recall that originally, one of the core growth stories for YETI was regional expansion within the U.S. YETI had a strong presence in the southeast and eastern seaboard markets where it originated, but penetration was relatively lower in the west and Midwest markets. Clearly, however, there may not be as much white space for expansion as YETI had originally hoped for. This sales weakness is also a reflection of weaker wholesale demand, which ultimately impacts the company’s overall brand visibility.

Counterbalancing this sales cut is a two-point upswing in expected operating margins, which is a partial statement of a richer DTC/full-price sales mix (versus wholesale revenue that incurs trade discounts) as well as YETI’s own opex management. Underlying EPS is now expected at $2.34-$2.48, the midpoint of which is ~20% richer than the prior expectation (helped in part as well by a more favorable tax rate), but we have to ask ourselves: Despite the guidance boost to earnings, do we really think YETI is worth buying when it has cut its growth expectations for two straight quarters?

At current share prices near $33, YETI trades at a 14x FY25 P/E ratio. On paper, that valuation appears cheap against an S&P 500 that has stubbornly clung to a >20x earnings multiple. That said, I find it difficult to view YETI as anything more than a challenging value trap. While we note that YETI maintains an impressive pace of product innovation (on track to release 30 new products this year), sluggish top-line demand suggests the company has tapped out near the top of its market potential, and “newness” isn’t enough to drive new and returning consumers back to the fold.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q2 recap

Let’s now go through YETI’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

YETI’s revenue declined 3.8% y/y to $445.9 million, vastly missing Wall Street expectations of $461.7 million (-0.4% y/y) by a 3-point margin.

A couple of drivers are responsible for YETI’s decay. The first is decaying U.S. trends. Revenue in Yeti’s domestic market fell 5% y/y, decelerating from a 2% y/y decline in Q1.

This is a reflection of a weaker consumer spending environment in the U.S. We also can’t ignore the fact that YETI’s core products are quite hardy. A cooler well cared for can last a very long time. YETI’s relative inability to re-inspire sales growth in the U.S., to me, is a rather clear indication that it’s not facing a brand awareness problem, but a saturation problem, especially when the company sits in a product category with virtually no moat and other very popular competitors like Stanley in the mix.

To some extent, international is helping to prop YETI up. But with only 2% y/y growth and with international representing only ~20% of the company’s revenue, the company’s weakening performance in the United States is a much bigger drag.

The second factor is a continued and very worrying drag in wholesale. YETI saw a 7% y/y decline in wholesale revenue in Q2, which is incredibly disappointing in the fact that it decelerated 8 points versus slight 1% growth in Q1.

YETI’s failure to grow wholesale is also a reflection of the cautious consumer environment. Naturally, retailers and wholesalers are expressing caution and unwillingness to take on inventory, especially when there’s a chance they could be stuck holding product through the critical holiday sales season.

The company has commented on several concerning trends that popped up in the quarter. First, the retail environment has grown increasingly promotional, with brands turning to discounts to clear product (a sentiment echoed across other consumer products companies as well). On top of this, consumers are “trading down” to cheaper products. Per CEO Matt Reintjes’ remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

While top line demand in Q2 came in slightly below expectations, reflecting ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and cautious behavior from consumers and our retail partners. We believe the actions we're taking position us extremely well for long-term sustainable growth in both revenue and earnings (…)

In Drinkware, while the market remained highly promotional and as we have discussed, continues to shift away from the recent trend-driven growth, we remain focused on broadening our portfolio in sustainable innovation while maintaining our discipline on high-quality, profitable growth (…)

Higher-priced soft coolers were more challenged in the quarter as we saw some evidence of consumer trade down. While we expect the higher end products to be staples of our portfolio long term, we are pleased with our strategy to capture a consumer up and down the price and performance ladder.

As reflected in the company’s guidance update, YETI has managed to partially offset sales weakness via margin progress. Pro forma gross margins improved 10bps y/y to 57.8%. The company’s sales mix (toward full-price DTC and away from wholesale), plus “selective” price increases to offset tariffs, have helped here.

The company has also done a good job at trimming back opex, with YETI cutting selling, general, and administrative expenses by 2% y/y. Still, it has been difficult to overcome sales weakness: adjusted EPS fell 6% y/y to $0.66, though this did beat Street expectations of $0.55 despite the top-line miss.

Upside opportunities and key takeaways

Of course, there are also potential upside drivers that we should keep in mind. YETI noted that sell-through (end customer demand) outpaced sell-in during Q2 in the United States, despite wholesale revenue (sell-in) weakness. If sell-through continues to outpace wholesale shipments, retailers may be induced to make replenishment orders, which would help to reverse current weak wholesale trends. This, on top of YETI’s low P/E ratio, could provide upside if the market does a more complete rotation away from growth and to value stocks.

That said, I’m skeptical about YETI’s future given the questionable sales trends and weak retail environment. In my view, this stock is likely to be dead money in the near to medium term. Steer clear for now and invest elsewhere.

data.title: FSTA: Consumer Staples Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series provides a top-down analysis of the consumer staples sector based on industry metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Fidelity MSCI Consumer Staples Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FSTA).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios ((EY, SY, FY)) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios ((ROE, GM)) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.
